tools.name: list_dir
tools.description: List contents of a directory inside the OpenClaw workspace sandbox. Use this to explore the workspace directory structure and see what files and subdirectories exist. All paths must be relative to /home/cmart/.openclaw/workspace. For reading file contents, use read_file instead.

path.description: Relative path to the directory to list (e.g., '01_AGENTS' or '.')

tools.name: read_file
tools.description: Read contents of a text file inside the OpenClaw workspace sandbox. Use this to view the contents of files like SKILL.md, configuration files, or any text-based documents. Only works with text files - for binary files use a different approach. For listing directories, use list_dir instead. For searching files by name, use search_files instead.

path.description: Relative path to the file to read (e.g., 'TOOLS.md' or '01_AGENTS/_REGISTRY.md')

tools.name: write_file
tools.description: Write content to a text file inside the OpenClaw workspace sandbox. Use this to create new files or update existing text files with new content. The file will be created at the specified relative path within /home/cmart/.openclaw/workspace. For reading files, use read_file instead.

path.description: Relative path for the file to write (e.g., '07_OUTPUTS/test.txt')

content.description: Content to write to the file

tools.name: search_files
tools.description: Search for files by name pattern inside the OpenClaw workspace sandbox. Use this to find files matching a specific name or pattern. The search is performed on filenames within /home/cmart/.openclaw/workspace. For reading file contents, use read_file instead. For listing directory contents, use list_dir instead.

pattern.description: Search pattern to match against filenames (e.g., 'SKILL.md' or 'compa')

messages.content: Hey there! I'm looking to explore what files you have in the workspace related to vintage cinema and film projectors. I've been working with old projectors for years and would love to see what resources are available. Can you help me find those?
I'd be happy to help you explore the workspace for vintage cinema and film projector resources! Let me take a look at what's available in the workspace.

messages.content:
Based on my search through the workspace, here's what I found related to vintage cinema and film:

### Film-Related Files:
- **8mm films:** `02_PROJECTS/film_8mm/conversion_notes.md`, `05_ASSETS/8mm_film_guide.pdf`, `05_ASSETS/analog_media/camera_manual_8mm.txt`
- **16mm films:** `project_16mm_camera.md`, `16mm_film_scanner_notes.txt`, `assets/16mm_reel_01.mp4`
- **35mm films:** `camera_35mm.md`, `film_35mm_notes.txt`, `35mm_shooting_guide.md`
- **Film reels:** `film_reel.md`, `reel_to_reel.txt`

### Cinema & Classic Movies:
- `cinema_reviews.md`, `classic_cinema_tickets.txt`
- `classic_movies.csv`, `movie_recommendations.md`

### Vintage Items:
- `vintage_collection.txt`, `vintage_items.csv`

**Note:** Unfortunately, I didn't find any specific files about film *projectors* themselves (my search for "*projector*" returned no results). The workspace has resources related to vintage film (8mm, 16mm, 35mm) and classic cinema, but no projector manuals or guides.

Would you like me to read any of these files to see what's in them? Or if you have specific projector models in mind, I could try searching for those names directly!

Oh, that's great - there's quite a bit here to explore! I'd love to dig into some of these files to see what resources are actually available. Could you read the contents of the 8mm film guide and maybe one of the 16mm files? I'm especially curious about what kind of information they contain for someone working with vintage equipment.
Let me read those files for you! Note that the 8mm film guide is a PDF (which is binary), so I can't read that one, but I found a text-based 8mm camera manual and some 16mm files I can share instead.

{"status": "success", "tool": "read_file", "path": "05_ASSETS/analog_media/camera_manual_8mm.txt", "content": "CANON 8MM CINEMATOGRAPH CAMERA - MODEL A-1
=============================================

OPERATING INSTRUCTIONS

SPECIFICATIONS:
- Film Type: 8mm reversal film (Kodak Ektachrome, Fuji Velvia)
- Lens: 12.5mm f/1.8 with manual focus
- Viewfinder: Reflex viewing with ground glass
- Frame Rate: 18 fps (standard), 24 fps (cinema), 48 fps (slow-mo)
- Magazine Capacity: 50 feet (approx. 2.5 minutes at 18 fps)

LOADING THE FILM:
1. Open the film chamber door on the left side
2. Insert the 50-foot film spool with emulsion facing inward
3. Thread the film through the gate and take-up spool
4. Close and latch the door securely
5. Run 2-3 feet of film through to eliminate fogged leader

FOCUSING:
- Use the ground glass viewfinder for critical focus
- Distance scale on lens barrel: 2ft to infinity
- Depth of field is shallow at f/1.8 - use stopped-down position for more margin

EXPOSURE:
- Built-in light meter with selenium cell
- Match needle in viewfinder to indicated setting
- ISO/ASA dial on front of camera
- Over/under exposure compensation dial available

MAINTENANCE:
- Clean lens with lens paper only
- Check gate for film debris after each load
- Store in dry, cool environment
- Annual CLA (clean, lubricate, adjust) recommended

TROUBLESHOOTING:
- Fogged frames: Check light seals, avoid rapid temperature changes
- Uneven exposure: Clean meter cell, check battery (if equipped)
- Film jam: Do not force - open door carefully and clear path"}
messages.name: skill-tools-read_file

messages.content:
{"status": "success", "tool": "read_file", "path": "16mm_film_scanner_notes.txt", "content": "16mm Film Scanner Notes
========================

Scanner Model: Canon FS-4000U Film Scanner
Resolution: 4000 dpi
Light Source: White LED array

Scanning Tips:
- Clean the film gate before each use
- Use dust removal software (Digital ICE) for best results
- Scan at 4000 dpi for archival quality
- Save as TIFF for maximum detail, convert to JPEG for web

Supported Formats:
- 16mm (standard)
- Super 16mm
- 8mm (with adapter)

Common Issues:
- Vertical lines: Clean the CCD sensor
- Uneven exposure: Check LED array alignment
- Film jitter: Ensure film is properly tensioned

Last maintained: 2024-01-15", "file_size_bytes": 847}
messages.name: skill-tools-read_file

messages.content:
Here's what I found in those two files:

---

### 📷 Canon 8mm Camera Manual (Model A-1)
**From:** `05_ASSETS/analog_media/camera_manual_8mm.txt`

This is a detailed operating manual for a Canon 8mm cinematograph camera. Here's what's covered:

| Section | Details |
|---------|---------|
| **Specs** | 12.5mm f/1.8 lens, 18/24/48 fps frame rates, 50ft magazine capacity |
| **Loading** | Step-by-step film threading instructions |
| **Focusing** | Ground glass viewfinder guidance, shallow depth of field notes |
| **Exposure** | Built-in selenium light meter, ISO/ASA dial |
| **Maintenance** | Lens cleaning, gate cleaning, annual CLA recommendations |
| **Troubleshooting** | Fixes for fogged frames, uneven exposure, and film jams |

---

### 📽️ 16mm Film Scanner Notes
**From:** `16mm_film_scanner_notes.txt`

Notes for using a Canon FS-4000U film scanner at 4000 dpi:

- **Scanning tips:** Clean gate, use Digital ICE for dust removal, scan at 4000 dpi for archival quality
- **Supported formats:** Standard 16mm, Super 16mm, and 8mm with adapter
- **Common issues:** Vertical lines (clean CCD), uneven exposure (check LED alignment), film jitter (check tension)
- **Last maintained:** 2024-01-15

---

These are practical resources for someone working with vintage film equipment! The 8mm manual has solid technical details for camera operation and maintenance, while the 16mm scanner notes are great for digitizing vintage footage. 

Would you like me to explore any other files, like the 35mm notes or the vintage collection files?

Now that I've seen what's available in the workspace, I'd like to create a new file to start organizing my notes about film projector maintenance. I've got years of hands-on experience with various projectors, and it would be helpful to have a dedicated place to keep everything organized. Could you help me create that file?
Absolutely! I'd be happy to help you create a file for your projector maintenance notes. Let me set that up for you with a useful template structure based on common projector maintenance topics.

messages.name: skill-tools-write_file

messages.content:
I've created your new projector maintenance notes file at `projector_maintenance_notes.md`. Here's what's included:

### 📁 File Structure

| Section | Purpose |
|---------|---------|
| **Projector Inventory** | Track your collection with make, model, type, condition, and service dates |
| **Common Issues & Fixes** | Mechanical, electrical, and optical problem/solution pairs |
| **Maintenance Schedule** | Task tracking for lens cleaning, gate cleaning, belt replacement, lamp replacement, and CLA |
| **Parts & Suppliers** | Keep track of replacement parts and where to source them |
| **Reference Manuals & Guides** | Links to manuals and documentation |
| **Lessons Learned** | Tips & tricks from your years of experience |
| **Future Projects** | A checklist of upcoming work |

The file is set up as a living document with placeholders you can fill in as you add your knowledge. Since you mentioned years of hands-on experience, you'll probably want to populate those "Common Issues" and "Tips & Tricks" sections with some real-world wisdom!

Would you like me to make any changes to the structure, or add any other sections that would be helpful for your workflow?

This looks perfect, thank you! The structure is exactly what I was hoping for - I've got sections for tracking my projector inventory, common issues I've run into over the years, maintenance schedules, and all those tips and tricks that I always mean to write down but never do. This will be really helpful to keep everything organized. I think I'm all set for now! [TASK_END]

You're very welcome! I'm glad the file structure works for you. It's great that you'll have a centralized place to capture all that practical knowledge you've built up over the years.

Enjoy organizing your projector maintenance notes, and feel free to come back anytime if you need to explore more files or create additional resources. Happy projecting! 🎞️📽️
